title: A Carpenter and a Sharecropper's Daughter
topic: Family, Farm, and the Move to Greenwood
summary: Interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi, in March 2013, Rosie Head opens on her birth in Holmes County to a father who was both carpenter and sharecropper, a working farm with cows, horses, mules, chickens, and hogs, a family of two brothers and seven sisters in which she fell about sixth, and the move from Lexington to Greenwood at six, where the children chopped and picked cotton from age seven.

title: One Room, One Teacher, Eighth Grade
summary: She describes missing first grade because the Hickory Springs school in Lexington lay across a creek she was too little to cross, then walking miles to a one-room church school in Greenwood where a single teacher taught every grade through eighth, and her love of reading.

title: She Had to Call Her Miss Corrine
summary: Head recalls carrying her shoes and washing her dusty legs on the hot walk to Sunday school, then becoming aware of segregation in Greenwood, her mother cooking for the plantation owner, having to address a little white girl named Corrine as Miss Corrine, being allowed to play outside but never enter the house, and a family that talked at home so the children knew the rules.

title: You Came Out in the Hole Again
summary: She describes the family picking seventy-five to eighty bales of cotton a year yet being told at the year-end settlement that they cleared nothing, her father returning from the plantation owner's office to tell her mother they came out in the hole again, the company store advancing twelve people thirty-five dollars a month, and her hurt that the family was cheated of the Christmas they looked forward to.

title: She Heard It on the Radio
summary: Head recounts hearing of the Emmett Till lynching on the family radio while her mother cooked, the killing tied to a store near her school across the railroad where the children spent pennies and nickels, the storekeeper who watched them as if they would steal, and a boy about her own age whose death made the children realize it could be them.

part: The Car and the Move to Tchula
title: He Laid His Keys Down on the Counter
summary: Head tells how, in the late 1950s, her father saved to buy a new car, and how the plantation boss, who wanted the men to send their wives to collect their pay, withheld her father's check when he refused to send her mother, seized the car keys off the counter, and claimed the car, prompting her father to use a spare set of keys.

part: The Car and the Move to Tchula
title: We Left That Plantation That Night
summary: She describes the family fleeing the plantation that night for Tchula, where an aunt lived, the boss scaring the neighbor holding their furniture into giving it up, losing the car when the man cut off her father's work and credit, getting one of the project houses near Mileston, and at seventeen picking and chopping cotton by the day for a dollar fifty.

title: A Way I Knew Was Better
summary: Head recounts first hearing in 1964 from Greenwood people about outsiders coming to help Mississippians register, attending meetings in Greenwood until a young man came to Tchula and they began their own meetings and a freedom school in a church, and explains that years of hearing her father say something had to be done, plus knowing how the people in charge treated them, drew her in.

title: The SNCC People in Greenwood
summary: Head names the SNCC organizers she encountered around Greenwood, including a worker she calls John Ball, Stokely Carmichael, Bob Moses, Lawrence Guyot, and Hollis Watkins, and describes meetings spent talking about segregated schools, courthouses, water fountains, and restaurants, that Black people were second class citizens paying first class taxes, and that the vote was where the power lay.

title: He Was Fearless
summary: Head contrasts two local leaders: the man whose name she renders as Turnbow, fearless, who would rouse a crowd, sip from his bowl during an armed standoff a visiting nurse witnessed, and predict the prisons that would fill with young Black men; and a quieter farmer who thought everything out and hung back to bail people out of jail. She also recalls hearing Medgar Evers speak and feeling his assassination like a family death.

title: A Mock Election to Prove the Point
summary: Head describes her work in the fall 1963 Freedom Vote, the mock election with Aaron Henry and Ed King meant to prove Black Mississippians would vote if given the chance, canvassing every house across Holmes County for the vote and for an integrated Head Start program needed to draw federal money, people afraid of eviction, white farmers who cursed canvassers off their property, and polling places in Thornton and Mileston.

title: They Would Shoot Up in There
summary: Head describes Klan activity in Holmes County, nights when about six of them worked late on newsletters at the Holmes County Community Center while trucks circled the building shouting threats and firing into it, and how a lookout across the railroad track gave just enough warning for the night riders to flee.

title: Almost Twenty-Four Hours a Day
summary: Head recalls overcoming constant fear through prayer, then the 1964 summer project that brought hundreds of mostly white Northern students to Mississippi, the roughly thirty volunteers in Holmes County, and her near round-the-clock canvassing. She recounts a march from Valley State toward Jackson pelted with rocks from boxcars, a man called Sam House arrested repeatedly, and a boycott march where police pulled a woman down in the street.

title: We Couldn't Believe It
summary: She recalls a man from California named Abe Osheroff coming to build the Mileston Community Center, her father helping construct it, the disbelief at strangers volunteering, neighbors gathering to watch the building go up, and the harassment that followed when authorities refused gas and telephone service and the highway patrol ticketed people crossing the railroad to reach it.

title: Everybody Took a Liking to Sue and Henry
topic: The Organizing Couple and the Center
summary: Head describes the couple she calls Sue and Henry settling in once they saw what the community needed and becoming beloved fixtures, Henry urging her to manage the center as they prepared to leave and telling her she should write a book, and her own work keeping all the records, taking messages from across and outside the state, sending newsletters, and supervising about four other young women.

title: A Little Room Built for the Clinic
topic: Bringing Health Care to the County
summary: Head recounts the medical workers, among them the nurse Josephine Disparti and a woman she names as Helen, who gave physical exams at the center until a small clinic room was built and people came from all over the county, the Medical Committee for Human Rights and a young doctor she calls Albert, a beginning she ties to comprehensive community health centers, and a childhood of home remedies with no Black doctors, when she never saw a doctor until her first child.

title: That's the Best School in the World
summary: Head describes setting up a makeshift kindergarten at the center two years before federal money arrived, with help from Sue and Henry and a truck Abe Osheroff left behind, driving around to collect children, feeding them grits and oatmeal, sewing their clothes, and a boy called Short who cried not to come but came to call it the best school in the world because it fed you and let you rest.

title: Dogs at the Courthouse Door
summary: Head recounts trying to register before the Voting Rights Act, going to the courthouse with five women where the sheriff and deputies came out with dogs, clubs, and guns, the dogs walked deliberately close, and being shut alone in a closet-like room with a desk to fill out the paperwork.

title: They Never Told Us We Had Passed
summary: She describes a dog left growling to guard the door so she was held some four hours, a chancery clerk who told her to go home and do as her mother and grandmother had, and the registrars never telling applicants they passed until federal registrars came after the 1965 Voting Rights Act, found the records, and revealed they had passed all along.

title: Truckloads to the Registrar
summary: Head describes hauling truckloads of fearful people to register because few would go on their own, the long coaxing it took, and the teachers and preachers who at first stayed away, thinking they stood in good with white folks or fearing for their jobs, until a teacher she calls Bernice Montgomery came early and hosted meetings at her house.

title: Transportation Available to You
summary: Head explains mobilizing voters for the 1966 congressional race with a candidate she calls Mr. Hayes, arranging gas and cars, posting people at the center to take calls, finding someone in each area to count how many needed rides, and organizing it all under the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party rather than the regular Democratic Party.

title: He Came In Drunk and Cursing
summary: Head describes serving as receiving and returning manager, a circuit clerk pressuring her to keep doing the work after a new white woman was given her title, and a log-truck driver who came drunk and cursing to drag his wife out of the polling place until local people removed him, plus the long bitter-cold counts that ran to three or four in the morning.

title: Looking Over Your Shoulder
summary: Head recalls rough-looking white men parking at the door and standing over the count, poll watching she did in Canton where the sight of such men turned Black families' cars away, helping voters who could not read by directing them to authorized assistants, and a woman who, after asking her for help, was told she could not vote and then had her food stamps and welfare check cut off until it was straightened out.

title: Everybody Knew Robert Clark
summary: Head describes the 1967 local elections and Robert Clark's race for the state legislature, a well-known former schoolteacher whom whites tried everything to defeat, the MFDP stationing helpers inside and outside every polling place against intimidation, the word-of-mouth network that told neighbors which candidates to support, and Clark becoming the first African American in the state legislature in the twentieth century.
related_events: Election of Robert Clark to Mississippi Legislature

title: The Only Center in the Program
summary: Head recounts the Child Development Group of Mississippi Head Start, brought to them by Sue and Henry, a six-week funded program for which Mileston was the only center, traveling to Mount Beulah for a week of federal training, working with Polly Greenberg and Tom Levin, and serving as secretary and assistant teacher while driving the payroll to Jackson each week.

title: The Bank Wouldn't Cash Our Checks
summary: She describes the Tchula bank refusing to cash CDGM checks until a supermarket owner talked them into it, white merchants she names as Cohen and others pressing the group at a night meeting to let them take over the funds and being refused, overhearing those men say on the stairs that the program would fail because Black people could not handle money, and the first six weeks ending with officials coming to take the furniture.

title: They Had Never Seen a Shower
summary: Head describes the program once funds came, restaurant-quality meals of steak and pork chops, equipment donated by farmers and carpenters, teaching children who had never seen a shower or indoor toilet, writing down and reading back the things they knew, and a graduation where teachers could not believe the children could write and recognize their names.

title: They Said It Was a Civil Rights Outfit
summary: Head recounts CDGM losing its funding despite being judged the best program in the country, attributed to Senator John Stennis and others calling it a civil rights outfit, the people working all over again like the movement, and a busload of children and a teacher she names as Zellma traveling to Washington to lobby for Head Start funds.

title: Still There After Seven Years
summary: Head closes by recounting working three months without funds until refunding came the next May, her years of schooling since the movement, a two-year degree in 1978 and a CDA credential, repeated returns to college as requirements changed, health setbacks as a diabetic that left her a few hours short of graduating, and the interviewer's thanks on behalf of the Smithsonian.
